title: scenario_fire_home_oxygen_tanks
description: Residential fire with medical oxygen tanks inside — BLEVE risk

timeline.title: Dispatch
timeline.description: House fire reported. Neighbor advises the homeowner uses medical oxygen tanks inside the home.

timeline.title: En Route
timeline.description: Smoke from multiple windows visible. BLEVE risk communicated to all units.

timeline.title: On Scene
timeline.description: Active fire in the living room. Confirmed medical oxygen tanks inside. Homeowner evacuated — says three full tanks are near the couch.

timeline.title: BLEVE Warning
timeline.description: Crew hears hissing and sees a tank near the fire. Immediate BLEVE risk assessment required.

timeline.title: Defensive Shift
timeline.description: All crews withdrawn to safe standoff distance. Master stream applied from cover.

timeline.title: Fire Controlled
timeline.description: Fire knocked from defensive position. Tanks vented but no BLEVE occurred. Scene secured.

node-1.title: Oxygen Tank Hazard Recognition
node-1.situation: You arrive to a living room fire with confirmed medical oxygen tanks inside near the fire. The oxygen-enriched atmosphere will cause dramatically accelerated fire spread. An audible hissing indicates one tank valve may be open. The homeowner is safely outside.
node-1.question: How do you approach this fire?

options.label: A
options.text: Recognize the BLEVE and oxygen-enriched fire hazard — do not make interior entry toward the tanks. Establish a minimum 300-foot standoff, withdraw all crews, and prepare for a defensive attack with master streams from cover.

options.label: B
options.text: Make rapid entry to remove the oxygen tanks from the fire area since that removes the hazard.

options.label: C
options.text: Advance the attack line normally and knock the fire quickly before the tanks fail.

node-2.title: Defensive Positioning
node-2.situation: All crews have withdrawn. Master streams are being set up. The fire is intensifying due to the oxygen enrichment. You hear a loud pop — a small relief valve has activated on one tank. No BLEVE yet. You must direct water application from a protected position.
node-2.question: How do you direct the water application?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply water to cool the tanks and surrounding area from outside the structure using master streams operated from behind cover — focus water on the area where tanks are located to prevent pressure buildup.

options.label: B
options.text: Use a smaller handline to get closer to the fire for better accuracy since master streams waste water.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait for the oxygen to burn off before applying water — water on burning oxygen is dangerous.

node-3.title: Post-Event Safety Check
node-3.situation: The fire has been knocked down. All hissing has stopped. The tanks are visible in the debris — two appear intact but scorched. One appears to have vented. Before any crew enters the structure, you must ensure tank stability.
node-3.question: How do you clear the scene for entry?

options.label: A
options.text: Request hazmat team or fire investigator to assess the tanks before entry. Do not allow crew entry until tanks are confirmed safe or removed by qualified personnel. Monitor with thermal imager from outside.

options.label: B
options.text: Send one crew member in quickly to physically move the tanks outside so the rest of the crew can do overhaul.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait 30 minutes then allow normal overhaul entry since enough time has passed for any danger to pass.

node-4.title: Scenario Complete
node-4.situation: Hazmat personnel assessed and safely removed the oxygen tanks. All three tanks were found to have vented properly through pressure relief valves — no BLEVE occurred. Overhaul was completed safely after tank removal. The homeowner's oxygen provider was notified to deliver replacement equipment. Well done — your recognition of oxygen-enriched fire dynamics, early defensive posture, and proper hazmat coordination protected all personnel.
node-4.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
